segments.title: Sea Flight and the Fleet Air Arm's global war
segments.summary: Sea Flight uses one pilot's wartime recollections to show how Fleet Air Arm squadrons escorted convoys and fought from carriers around the world.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends Sea Flight as Hugh Popham's personal account of training and wartime service as a Hurricane pilot with the Fleet Air Arm. Its itinerary stretches from North Atlantic convoy routes and the West Indies to Ceylon and Madagascar, showing how widely carrier aviation operated during the Second World War. The quoted passages move between routine patrols, aircraft returning with bullet damage, a missing Albacore crew and a low-level attack under fire. The book's later chapters continue through HMS Illustrious, Campania and Striker, giving the memoir a broader carrier-service perspective than one ship or campaign. This watch point makes the book a route into both the daily hazards and global reach of British naval aviation.

evidence.note: The review describes a Fleet Air Arm memoir, its convoy-patrol setting, a combat episode, and its contents.

segments.title: Carrier aircraft and Royal Marines force a harbour decision
segments.summary: A combat passage from Sea Flight shows carrier aircraft, destroyers and Royal Marines combining to overcome a defended harbour during the Madagascar fighting.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke reads a Sea Flight episode in which an enemy sloop remains able to cover a harbour and obstruct troops ashore. Six Hurricanes from HMS Indomitable make a low-level strafing attack through smoke and return fire, despite doubts about what rifle-calibre machine-gun bullets can do against the ship's plating. The aircraft leave the sloop burning, but air attack is only one part of the combined action. HMS Anthony then enters the harbour under fire and lands fifty Royal Marines, who seize the naval depot, while HMS Diamond follows in. The passage is a compact illustration of carrier aviation, surface ships and landing troops producing an operational result together.

evidence.note: The quoted memoir passage describes the sloop, six Hurricanes attacking under fire, Anthony landing fifty Royal Marines, Diamond entering, and the harbour action ending.

segments.title: Ark Royal's career, design debate and wreck record
segments.summary: A history of HMS Ark Royal follows the carrier from development to loss while opening questions about how her design should be interpreted.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends Mike Rossiter's Ark Royal as a full career history, covering the carrier's operations and voyages from beginning to end. He values its pictures, layout and even-handed treatment of aircraft such as the Skua and Swordfish. At the same time, he cautions that he does not wholly share the book's standardized account of the ship's development and design, reminding readers that a comprehensive narrative can still embody a debatable interpretation. Photographs of the search for Ark Royal and underwater Swordfish and Fulmar wreckage connect the written history to the physical evidence surviving around the wreck.

evidence.note: The review assesses the Ark Royal history, its design treatment, and photographs connected with the wreck search.

segments.title: Swordfish, Taranto and carrier-landing culture
segments.summary: A Swordfish history of the Taranto raid brings carrier aviation's operational effect together with the humour and hazards of wartime deck landings.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke praises David Wragg's Swordfish history of the Taranto raid for its exceptional images, lively writing and quotations from both sides of the conflict. A dark Fleet Air Arm joke about whether a pilot can swim conveys the fatalism surrounding a bad carrier landing at sea. The book's selected voices therefore illuminate service culture as well as the raid's operational history. Dr. Clarke also highlights Andrew Cunningham's barbed recollection of a lavish pre-war visit with Admiral Riccardi, placing personality, professional judgment and retrospective wartime criticism beside the later attack on the Italian fleet.

evidence.note: The review discusses the Swordfish book, its Taranto subject, a Fleet Air Arm landing joke, and the Cunningham-Riccardi anecdote.

segments.title: Naval Wings: a Swordfish pilot's wider war
segments.summary: Naval Wings follows a Fleet Air Arm Swordfish pilot from Mediterranean strikes to the Atlantic and the war against Japan.
segments.body: The review introduces John Wellham's Naval Wings through his service with 824 Squadron aboard HMS Eagle. Wellham flew in the Swordfish attack on Bomba in which three torpedoes sank four ships, an episode used to demonstrate the growing power of aircraft in naval warfare. He was then selected for the second strike on Taranto from HMS Illustrious. The memoir continues through Atlantic and anti-submarine operations to the final campaign against Japan with the British Pacific Fleet, showing how one Fleet Air Arm career crossed several theatres. This watch point is useful for following the operational range of the Swordfish beyond its most famous raid.

evidence.note: The review outlines Wellham's Swordfish service, the memoir's theatres, and its account of personal memory checked against records.

segments.title: How Naval Wings checks memory against official records
segments.summary: John Wellham explains how taped recollections, flying logbooks, photographs and official records were combined to create Naval Wings without hiding their disagreements.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke reads John Wellham's explanation of how Naval Wings grew from taped recollections encouraged by the Fleet Air Arm Museum's curator and by questions from Wellham's family. Wellham describes the book as a personal record in his own words rather than an attempt to replace existing operational histories. He supported memory with his flying logbook and contemporary photographs, while dates and squadron numbers were checked against official records. Crucially, he notes that those records sometimes disagreed and that the museum helped investigate disputed points. The passage gives readers a concise lesson in using a participant memoir as evidence: its first-person value remains substantial, but memory and documentation both require comparison.

evidence.note: Wellham's preface explains the taped-memory origins, use of logbooks and photographs, official-record checks, disagreements, and Fleet Air Arm Museum assistance.

segments.title: Flying through Red Sea mirage conditions
segments.summary: A Naval Wings passage shows how painted petrol drums helped pilots judge a desert runway when continuous mirage obscured height and distance.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke closes with Wellham's account of reaching a Red Sea airfield after an eight-hour flight. Near-continuous mirage made it difficult for pilots to judge height over the sand, turning an apparently simple landing into a local navigation problem. Ground personnel marked virtual runway edges with black-painted petrol drums, using their alignment much like flare-path lights at night. The improvised system shows how air operations depended on environmental knowledge and practical ground support as much as on aircraft performance. Wellham's recollection also preserves the human aftermath of the flight, from the need for food and rest to the encounter with army officers who felt forgotten at the remote station.

evidence.note: The quoted Red Sea passage describes an eight-hour flight, mirage-obscured height judgment, black-painted petrol drums marking runways, and the remote airfield's accommodations.
